title: Bridge Essay: Displaying pride: Variation by social context, ethnic heritage, and gender?

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Wind/Pride** (Phase 4) with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge rationale:** Links Wind/Pride to mechanistic neuroscience.

**Displaying pride: Variation by social context, ethnic heritage, and gender?** — PLoS One. PMID: 37115772. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0285152. Pride is universal; however, the complexities linked to its social status functions and implications for social relationships suggest the possibility of variation in its display. Drawing from empirical evidence, this study examined whether displayed pride would vary by social context (i.e., whether the target was acompetitor or aloved one), ethnic heritage (i.e., membership in individualistic or collectivistic cultural groups) and by gender.
